title: Incised name sign in air-dry clay
subtitle: A clay slab with carved letters for a door, shelf, or garden sign
excerpt: An incised name sign is a clay slab with a word or name carved into the surface at leather-hard stage, pierced for hanging, and sealed once bone-dry. The carved letters are recessed below the surface and catch the light differently from the flat surround. Air-dry clay holds incised lettering well once sealed. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Incised slab sign technique. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness. The base for the sign.
The optimal stage for incised carving: firm enough for a clean cut, damp enough for the tool to move smoothly.
Carved into the clay surface, leaving grooves that read as darker lines against the flat surround.

content.text: of clay with lettering carved into its surface at

stage. Unlike a paper clay plaque with carved letters, this sign uses standard air-dry clay and keeps the letters simpler, block capitals with straight cuts that do not need the fibre reinforcement that paper clay provides. The finished sign reads clearly as a sign rather than decoration and works indoors on a shelf, door frame, or wall.

attrs.title: Block capitals
attrs.body: Use block capitals without serifs for a first sign. Serifs and curved letters (S, C, G) are harder to cut cleanly in clay. A five-letter word in 3 cm uppercase block letters fits a slab 22 x 8 cm with a 2 cm border. Plan the spacing on paper before cutting.

items.name: Pottery rolling pin and 8 mm guide rails
Sgraffito tool or firm dental pick
Thin cord or D-rings for hanging

content.text: Step 1: Roll and cut the slab

Roll the clay to 8 mm thick. Cut the sign to the dimensions needed for the text, with a 2 cm border around the lettering on all sides. A thicker slab (8 mm rather than 6 mm) gives the lettering more depth and makes the sign more robust.
Leave the slab on a flat board for two to three hours until leather-hard. The clay should hold its shape when lifted at one corner and feel firm to the touch without being tacky.
Step 3: Mark and carve the letters

Use the needle tool to scratch a baseline and a cap-height line across the slab as guides. Lightly sketch each letter within the guides. Carve each letter to a final depth of 3 to 4 mm using the sgraffito tool, pressing firmly and drawing in single confident strokes. Clean loose clay from the grooves with the needle tool tip. Straight strokes are easier than curved ones: H, E, F, I, L, T are good starting letters.

Pierce two hanging holes in the top edge of the sign, 3 cm from the left and right edges and 1.5 cm from the top. Alternatively, press two D-ring hardware pieces into the wet clay at the same positions and allow them to be encased as the clay dries, this is more secure than cord through holes for heavier signs.

Dry between two boards for the first four hours to keep the sign flat, then in open air until fully bone-dry. Sand rough spots. Seal with three coats of PVA diluted 1:1, signs are handled more than tiles and need the extra protection. A dark wash of acrylic paint worked into the carved grooves and wiped off the flat surface reads clearly as dark letters on a pale ground.
Add a simple border of stamped impressions around the edge to frame the lettering.
Paint the whole sign in a dark base colour and dry-brush a lighter highlight over the surface, the incised letters remain dark and read clearly.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems when making the incised name sign.

items.symptom: Carving tool skates over the surface without cutting
items.cause: Clay has dried past leather-hard and is approaching bone-dry.
items.fix: Mist the surface lightly with water, wait five minutes, and retry. At bone-dry, incising is still possible but requires more pressure and leaves a slightly rougher edge.

items.cause: One face dried faster than the other.
items.fix: Dry between two boards, weighted firmly, for the first eight hours. An 8 mm slab is more prone to warping than a 6 mm one but more robust once sealed.
